does: This paper adapts mechanistic interpretability techniques from Transformer research to investigate the Indirect Object Identification task in Mamba, a recent recurrent neural network architecture. The authors reverse-engineer the IOI circuit in Mamba by identifying Layer 39 as a critical bottleneck where convolutions shift token positions and name entities are stored linearly within the SSM component. They adapt positional Edge Attribution Patching, an automatic circuit discovery tool, to trace information flow through Mamba's architecture. The work provides concrete evidence that interpretability methods designed for Transformers can be successfully applied to fundamentally different architectural paradigms, establishing that circuit-based mechanistic interpretability generalizes beyond the Transformer family.
connects: This work extends the circuit-based mechanistic interpretability framework pioneered in Transformer research, particularly building on studies of the IOI task which has become a standard benchmark for understanding how models implement specific algorithmic behaviors. The authors draw on established techniques including ablation-based causal analysis and automated circuit discovery tools developed for Transformers. The research responds to the broader question of whether interpretability methods remain useful as model architectures diverge, directly addressing concerns about the generalizability of mechanistic interpretability findings. It engages with the growing literature on alternative architectures like state-space models and recurrent networks that challenge Transformer dominance.
enables: This work opens pathways for mechanistic understanding of non-Transformer architectures, which is increasingly important as diverse model families compete for adoption. By demonstrating that circuit-based interpretability tools transfer to Mamba, the findings suggest similar techniques could apply to other emerging architectures, enabling researchers to build interpretability toolkits that work across the model zoo. The paper establishes baselines for understanding how different architectural components—like convolutions and state-space mechanisms—implement algorithmic functions, which could inform the design of more interpretable future models. The approach also enables more targeted auditing and debugging of recurrent architectures used in production systems.
